entry.title: Assign splitting structure to newly created cost center
entry.content:
Dear Gurus,

Currently in production client, there are two splitting structures for cost centers under different company codes.

When I created new cost centers, the cost centers are automatically assigned to the splitting structure K0. However, I want my cost centers to be assigned to the splitting structure K1.

Is there any way I can assign the splitting structure to a group of cost centers?

or I have to request direct configuration every time I create new production cost centers?

entry.title: The Rise of Agentic AI: Transforming Enterprise Intelligence in SAP Ecosystems
entry.content:
Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is fundamentally reshaping how modern enterprises operate, innovate, and compete. What began as simple automation has now evolved into intelligent systems capable of learning, adapting, and making decisions.

Today, we are entering a transformative new phase the era of Agentic AI where AI systems no longer just respond to inputs but actively plan, reason, act, and collaborate to achieve complex business goals.

I remember when AI was mostly about crunching numbers for forecasts. Now, with SAP innovation,  it's stepping into full-blown independence. Companies using SAP S/4HANA stand out because they can weave these agents right into their daily grind, turning rigid processes into something flexible and smart

For SAP-driven organizations, this shift represents not just a technological upgrade, but a paradigm change in how business processes are executed, optimized, and orchestrated.

From Task Automation to Agentic Systems

The evolution of AI reflects a steady progression toward greater autonomy and intelligence. Each phase has contributed to building the foundation for today’s agent-driven ecosystems.

1. Rule-Based Systems: The Foundation of Automation

Early AI systems were deterministic and logic-driven. These rule-based systems relied on predefined conditions and workflows to execute tasks.

Examples included:

  • Basic chatbots
  • Workflow automation engines
  • Decision trees in ERP systems

While effective for structured tasks, these systems lacked adaptability and could not handle ambiguity or dynamic environments common in enterprise operations.

2. Machine Learning & Neural Networks: Learning from Data

The 1990s marked a significant shift toward data-driven intelligence with the rise of Machine Learning (ML) and Neural Networks.

That was a shift. Systems started pulling lessons from piles of data. Think IBM Watson showing off its thinking chops, or AlexNet flipping image analysis upside down with deep layers. What that meant was better guesses over time - spotting demand trends, sniffing out risks, keeping quality in check. In SAP, it popped up in supply chain modules or finance dashboards, making predictions feel less like guesswork.

Key breakthroughs included systems like:

  • IBM Watson, which demonstrated cognitive computing capabilities
  • AlexNet, which revolutionized image recognition through deep learning

These innovations enabled systems to:

  • Learn patterns from large datasets
  • Improve predictions over time
  • Support decision-making in areas like demand forecasting, risk analysis, and quality management

In SAP environments, this phase enabled predictive analytics and embedded intelligence within modules such as supply chain planning and finance.

3. Deep Learning, Generative AI & NLP: Human-Like Intelligence

Recent advancements in Deep Learning, Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Generative AI have accelerated AI adoption across enterprises.

Deep learning took it further, especially with natural language and generative tools. Large models now chat like people, whip up reports, or handle queries in apps. For SAP users, that translates to copilots that guide you through tasks or interfaces where you just talk it out. Insights come automatically, no digging required. Pretty handy when you're buried in data.

Large Language Models (LLMs) now enable systems to:

  • Understand and generate human-like language
  • Automate documentation and reporting
  • Provide conversational interfaces for enterprise applications

This has significantly enhanced user experience in SAP ecosystems through:

  • Intelligent copilots
  • Conversational AI interfaces
  • Automated insights generation

The Frontier: Agentic AI

But here's the exciting bit , agentic AI pulls all that together into something proactive. These agents don't wait for orders. They chase goals, weigh options in changing situations, loop in other agents or folks, and tweak as they go. No more hostile into narrow lanes. Instead, they orchestrate across tools, data, everything.

All these advancements converge into the evolution: Agentic AI.

 

Agentic AI systems are characterized by their ability to:

  • Act autonomously toward defined goals
  • Reason and make decisions in dynamic environments
  • Collaborate with other agents and humans
  • Continuously learn and adapt

Unlike traditional AI, which operates within predefined boundaries, Agentic AI introduces goal-driven orchestration across systems, processes, and data.

Why Agentic AI Matters for SAP Enterprises

In the context of SAP S/4HANA and intelligent enterprise frameworks, Agentic AI unlocks new capabilities:

  • End-to-End Process Automation
    AI agents can manage complete workflows from demand sensing to order fulfillment without constant human intervention.
  • Intelligent Decision-Making
    Agents can evaluate multiple scenarios in real time, enabling proactive and optimized business decisions.
  • Cross-Functional Collaboration
    AI agents can interact across SAP modules (MM, SD, TM, Finance), breaking silos and improving process integration.
  • Adaptive Supply Chains
    Agentic systems can dynamically respond to disruptions, demand fluctuations, and logistics constraints. From what I've seen in SAP environments, this hits hard on a few fronts. End-to-end automation stands out. An agent might sense demand, route orders, and wrap fulfillment all on its own, cutting out the handoffs. Real issue tends to be human bottlenecks - this sidesteps them.

    Decision-making gets sharper too. Picture evaluating scenarios live, picking the best path without delay. Proactive moves like that optimize on the spot. And collaboration? Agents bridge modules - materials management, sales, transport, finance - smashing those old silos. Processes flow smoother because of it Supply chains adapt faster with this. Disruptions hit, demands swing, logistics snag - agents adjust dynamically. No more reactive scrambling. It's what actually happens in practice for businesses leaning on SAP. They build resilient setups that bend instead of break.

Conclusion

The journey from rule-based automation to Agentic AI represents a fundamental shift in enterprise intelligence. Organizations leveraging SAP platforms are uniquely positioned to harness this transformation by embedding AI agents into their core processes.

The way I see it, this evolution from basic rules to full agents marks a deep change. SAP outfits are primed for it, embedding these smarts into core ops. Thing is, as we push toward self-running systems, agentic AI drives the edge - efficiency ramps up, agility sharpens, and staying ahead gets easier in a fast digital world. Turns out, the ones who grab it first will pull away.

As businesses move toward autonomous, intelligent ecosystems, Agentic AI will become the cornerstone of innovation driving efficiency, agility, and competitive advantage in the digital era.

entry.title: Re: Default Price List Issue in Inventory Transfer
entry.content:
Hi Janeesh,

 

unfortunately there is no workaround, as it will always default to this setting in the header. Please note that the selected Price list in the header is only informative and the price shown from that price is for user information. Transaction will be made with actual item cost calculated by the system.

With that said, you can always use a workaround by giving them read-only permission to this pricelist, and changing the UI for those users to exclude the Price field. This way prevents them from seeing the price, while maintaining the possibility of using Inventory Transfer.

 

BR,

Matija

entry.title: Batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order with same WBS or same linked Sales order
entry.content:
Hi Members,

I have requirement to implement an automated invoicing routine that automatically identifies and uses the pair of Sales Order and Outbound to create the billing document. The routine will also update the Billing Document's output type EDI = Immediately and save it once again. 

This routine cannot take the customer PO as reference as in the past it created issues as it would combine all outbounds that were linked to the same customer PO and create a single invoice (lump sum).
This would cause rejection from our customer, as a single billing document per SO / Outbound is expected per delivery.
we need to introduce a batch invoicing job for delivery plus sales order with the same WBS element or same linked sales order for sales Org. 
 
I checked for "schedule billing creation" app but parameter on the app is not satisfied. 
 
Thanks and Regards,
Rajesh

entry.title: Re: Maximizing BTP Job Scheduling Service with SAP PAPM Cloud Universal Model
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Just wanted to share a couple of notes in case you run into the same issue:

1. Deployment of Custom Application (proxy)

I ran into an error message "BuildpackCompileFailed - App staging failed in the buildpack compile phase".

Issue was fixed by changing node from ^18 to ^20 in the package.json file

package.json

{
"dependencies": {
"@sap/xsenv": "^5.4.0",
"@sap/xssec": "^4.2.7",
"axios": "^1.7.9",
"express": "^4.21.2",
"http-proxy-middleware": "^3.0.3",
"jsonwebtoken": "^9.0.2"
},
"engines": {
"node": "^20",
"npm": "^9"
}
}

2. JSS ends with error 500 Internal Server Error

This issue turned out to be tied to the manifest.yml and User-Provided Variable (in Step 7). A few things that solved it for me:

  • Make sure that the BTP_USERNAME and BTP_PASSWORDbelongs to a valid technical user in your SAP BTP subaccount. This technical user needs both authorization: PaPM UM role (ace-ACE-live-prod!*) and role generated by the deployed proxy app (jba-application!*)
  • The tehnical user must be provisioned through an SAP Identity Provider (IdP). Using SAP IdP via SAP for me works fine too (C-user, P-user, S-user account)
  • Lastly, make sure that the BTP_USERNAME and BTP_PASSWORD values in both manifest.yml and the User-Provided Variable (in Step 7) match exactly, and confirm the pasword is valid. 

I hope this information helps cut down your troubleshooting time!

Regards,

Karen

entry.title: Re: Need correct path to update OT document number field...
entry.content:
Good day @MehmetBitiren01 

Thank you for your question.

For the OT Document Number field used for Poland SAF-T Fixed Assets, the system expects you to maintain this data in the fixed asset master record. You should also integrate the acquisition and retirement document numbers for the SAF-T fixed asset forms.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb525a45b55f4a1c9bb32c254affeeec/f87f1d12926f49128f78410f6738ae94.html?locale=en-US

However, for your specific mass update need, the Public Cloud does not offer a communication scenario or a field specific API endpoint published by SAP for OT Document Number alone. Mass processing of fixed assets is available through Asset Accounting APIs. See KBA 3625447 and 3219541 for more details.

So, the approach i recommend is:

  • Maintain the field manually in Managed Fixed Assets if your volume is small

  • Check the released Fixed Asset APIs in the API Business Hub if your volume is large
  • Only use an integration if the OT Document Number field is actually part of the released API contract

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Eligible earnings for salaried and hourly employees case
entry.content:
Hi,

Request for your expert opinion for my case below:

My client uses 31/12 annualized salary for Eligible Earnings for salaried employees from EC. For hourly employees and for mid cycle FTE change employees, they receive an excel file from payroll with eligible earning amounts per employee id. The bonus percent and employee history  time periods are maintained in EC itself.

I am exploring on an automation option for hourly and FTE changers , where instead of manually importing the earning in EH file, can we use MDF object to load the file received from payroll while keeping the setting of 'Point in Time' config for other salaried population.

Will it be possible to use 2 sources for salary field (eligible earning)  in the same template: 1. Annualized salary point in time 31/12 from pay component group for salaried population and 2. an MDF object for hourly and FTE changers.

 

Thanks,

Swagatika

entry.title: Re: Exploring AI-Driven Finance Use Cases: Seeking Inputs...
entry.content:
Hi, That is a great question. I would say: Follow the process and let's see:

Implementation

- Would be great if there is some kind of reporting tool that can analyse current reports (excel based or PDF from a legacy system), compares to the implemented data model and suggest a report in Group Reporting. (same for input forms in GRDC)

- Allignment and validation of historical data typically is very cumbersome. Would be great if, based on existing reports, AI can compare reported data, analyse differences and prepare journals to correct data.

- Synchronization of global hierarhcies with local hierarchies.

- Let AI suggest validation rules based on the data model.

Maintenance

- When masterdata is created somewhere in the landscape, automatically suggest: Masterdata fields, mappings (GL account to FS item), Hierarchies etc. 

- Authorisation request: Use AI to ask the right questions to a user to refine an authorisation request to the right roles and authorisation objects.

Use

- Changes in consolidation and ownership structures are rather incidental for companies. Would be great if you can just prompt: We sold 30% of the shares of company X and than changes to the applicable structures are suggested as well as bookings in BS and PL to register the transaction. 

- Internal audit. AI legally can not (yet) replace the work of an accountant / auditor. But what if AI already does the challanges  // checks / controls that a auditor will do and than create a report, suggest adjustments to be booked and so on just to make sure that when the real auditor comes, the road is already cleared. 

- Prompt based journal suggestions: I want a booking to accomplish this: and than the booking is suggested. Might even be used to suggest distribution / allocation of costs. If a method has been found and refined, it should be possible to store it as a task.

entry.title: Build Your First Agent Skill From Ground Zero - The Knowledge Gap Every LLM Has
entry.content:
Blog 5 of N: Agent Skills Changed How I Use Claude Code - Here's the Open Standard Behind It

What if your AI agent could learn a procedure once and remember it forever? Not facts - it already knows plenty of those. But the step-by-step, context-specific processes that make your team's work actually work. That is what agent skills do. And the format is simpler than you think.

What Are Agent Skills?

If you have used any AI coding tool recently - Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, or similar - you have probably noticed something. These tools are impressive reasoners. They can explain complex code, debug tricky issues, and generate entire applications from a description. The underlying large language models already know a massive amount of facts. Ask one about microservice architecture, the history of relational databases, or how HTTP works - you will get a solid, confident answer.

But there is a specific kind of knowledge they lack. And that gap is exactly what skills were created to fill.

The Missing Piece: Procedural Knowledge

Let's say your company has a weekly status report that every team member submits. It is not just any report - your team has a specific way of doing it:

  1. Start with completed items, listed as bullet points
  2. Then blockers - each one must have a severity rating (low, medium, high) and who owns the resolution
  3. Anything overdue by more than 2 days gets a risk flag
  4. Then next week's planned items
  5. Always end with a "Needs from Leadership" section, even if empty
  6. Bullet points only, no paragraphs, no filler

Six rules. Specific order. Specific formatting.

Now open any AI tool and say: "Write my weekly status report."

You will get a perfectly reasonable, generic template. It will have sections for accomplishments and goals. It might even look professional. But it will miss your team's specific rules. No severity ratings on blockers. No risk flags. No mandatory leadership section. Because the AI has never seen your team's process.

This is the difference between two types of knowledge:

  • Factual knowledge - What things are. "A status report summarizes work done and work planned." The AI already knows this.
  • Procedural knowledge - How things get done in a specific context. "Our team's status report follows these 6 rules in this order." The AI does not know this.

LLMs ship with enormous factual knowledge. They can tell you what a status report is, what a code review is, what an incident report is. But they do not know your process for writing one. They do not know the 15-step onboarding procedure for new developers at your company. They do not know the exact sequence of checks your team runs before deploying to production.

That kind of knowledge - step-by-step, context-specific, "here is how we actually do this" - is procedural knowledge. And without it, the AI has only two options when it encounters a task like this:

  1. You tell it every single step, every single time. All 6 rules, every Monday morning. Hope you remember them all. Hope your colleague remembers them too.
  2. It takes its best guess. Which will look plausible but miss half your team's requirements.

Neither is great. And this is the problem skills were designed to solve.

A skill is how you give an AI agent procedural knowledge - the step-by-step, context-specific instructions for how to do a particular job.

What Does a Skill Look Like?

The format is remarkably simple. A skill is a folder containing a file called SKILL.md. That is the minimum. A folder and a Markdown file.

.claude/
└── skills/
    └── weekly-status/
        ├── SKILL.md          # Required: metadata + instructions
        ├── scripts/          # Optional: executable code
        ├── references/       # Optional: additional documentation
        └── assets/           # Optional: templates, data files

Only the SKILL.md file is required. The three subdirectories - scripts/, references/, and assets/ - are optional. Most skills do not need them. We will talk about what they do later, but for now, just know that a skill can be as simple as a single Markdown file in a folder.

Inside the SKILL.md

The file has two parts: a small header at the top, and the instructions below it.

The header (called frontmatter) is a few lines of metadata wrapped between --- markers. It has two mandatory fields:

---
name: weekly-status
description: >
  Writes the team's weekly status report in the standard format.
  Use when the user asks to write, draft, or prepare a status
  report, weekly update, or weekly summary.
---

 

FieldWhat it does
nameIdentifies the skill. Lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only, max 64 characters. Must match the folder name. Cannot start or end with a hyphen
descriptionTells the agent what this skill does and when to use it. This is the most important line in the entire file - more on why in a moment

There are also optional fields you can add:

FieldWhat it does
licenseLicense for sharing (e.g., "Apache-2.0")
compatibilityEnvironment requirements (e.g., "Requires Python 3.12+")
metadataKey-value pairs for things like author name and version number
allowed-toolsPre-approved tools the skill may use (experimental)

Tools like Claude Code also extend the standard with additional frontmatter fields - for example, disable-model-invocation (only the user can trigger the skill, preventing the agent from auto-activating it), context: fork (run the skill in an isolated subagent), and more. These are tool-specific extensions. The core format - name, description, and body - works everywhere.

The body is everything below the header. It is written in plain Markdown - no special syntax, no programming language, just text. This is where the actual instructions live. Step-by-step procedures, rules, examples of good and bad output, edge cases, anything the agent needs to know to do the job correctly.

There are no restrictions on how you structure the body. Write it however makes the procedure clearest. The agent will read it like a document and follow along.

That is the entire format. A header that says "here is what I am and when to use me" and a body that says "here is how to do it." If you can write a checklist in a text file, you can write a skill.

Why the Description Matters So Much

The description field controls when the skill activates. The agent does not read the full instructions upfront - it reads only the name and description. Later, when you ask it to do something, it compares your request against all the skill descriptions and loads the matching one. This matching happens through the AI's own reasoning - not keyword matching, but genuine understanding of intent.

This is why a specific description matters:

  • Good: "Writes the team's weekly status report in the standard format. Use when the user asks to write, draft, or prepare a status report, weekly update, or weekly summary."
  • Bad: "Helps with reports."

The good description has trigger words that match how people naturally ask for this task. The bad one is so vague the agent might never activate it. Think of it like labeling a recipe card - "Grandma's Dal" is useful, "food" is not.

The Optional Folders

Most skills only need the SKILL.md file. But for complex procedures, the three optional directories add capabilities:

DirectoryWhat it holdsWhen it loads
scripts/Executable code (Python, Bash, JavaScript)When the agent decides to run it
references/Additional documentation, guides, specsWhen the agent needs more context
assets/Templates, sample files, dataWhen a step references them

The key thing: these folders do not load when the skill activates. They load only when the agent reaches a step that needs them. This is part of a larger loading strategy we need to talk about next.

The Context Pollution Problem

Here is a question that comes up naturally: what if you have a lot of skills?

Maybe your team has a skill for writing status reports, another for meeting notes, one for incident reports, one for onboarding new team members, one for code reviews, one for release notes, one for sprint retrospectives. That is seven skills. A large organization might have dozens. Some teams have over a hundred.

If the agent loaded all of those skills - every instruction, every reference file, every template - into memory at startup, it would use up most of its context window before anyone even asked a question. The context window is the amount of text an AI model can hold in its working memory at one time. Fill it up with skill instructions you are not using, and there is less room for the actual conversation. This is called context pollution - irrelevant information crowding out the information you actually need.

This is also why you cannot just put all your procedures into a single instructions file like CLAUDE.md. If you stuffed your status report procedure, your meeting notes procedure, your incident report template, your onboarding checklist, and your deployment runbook all into oneCLAUDE.md file, it would be thousands of lines long. And it would load into context on every single session - even when you are just fixing a typo in a README and none of those procedures are relevant.

Skills solve this with a loading strategy called progressive disclosure.

Progressive Disclosure: Load Only What You Need

Progressive disclosure works in three tiers. Each tier loads more information, but only when it is actually needed.

Tier 1 - Just the labels (at startup)

When the agent starts, it loads only the name and description from every installed skill. That is roughly a sentence or two per skill - maybe 50 words each. Even if you have 200 skills installed, the total overhead at startup is a few thousand words. That is a tiny fraction of modern context windows, which can hold tens of thousands of words.

Tier 2 - The full instructions (when the task matches)

When you ask the agent to do something that matches a skill's description, the agent pulls the full SKILL.md body into its context. Now it has the complete step-by-step procedure. The specification recommends keeping the body under 500 lines so that this loading remains efficient.

The matching itself happens through the AI's own reasoning. You do not need to use exact keywords - the model understands intent. If you say "prepare my weekly update" and the skill description mentions "weekly status report," the model recognizes these are the same thing.

Tier 3 - Supporting resources (only at the point of need)

Files in scripts/, references/, and assets/ load only when the agent reaches a step that specifically needs them. If step 5 of a procedure says "use the template in assets/report-template.md," that template loads when the agent gets to step 5 - not when the skill activates, and certainly not at startup.

The result: an agent starts each session with a lightweight index of everything it knows how to do. It pulls in detailed instructions only when you ask for something relevant. And it grabs supporting resources only at the moment a specific step requires them. Most of the time, most of your skills take up almost zero space in the agent's working memory.

This is why skills scale in a way that instruction files cannot.CLAUDE.md ,rules.md, and similar project-level files are always loaded, always taking up context. Skills are loaded on demand, taking up context only when they are being used.

Skills vs. Other Ways of Teaching Agents

Skills are not the only way to give an AI agent additional knowledge. If you have used AI coding tools, you have probably encountered other approaches. They all serve different purposes.

Instruction Files (CLAUDE.md,rules.md,.cursorrules) are project-level rules that load at startup and stay in context the entire session. Good for broad conventions like "always use TypeScript strict mode" or "never commit .env files." But if you stuff every procedure in there - status reports, meeting notes, incident templates, deployment checklists - it becomes thousands of lines that load every session, even when you are just renaming a variable. Think of it as a poster on the wall: good for a few important rules, bad for a filing cabinet of procedures.

Agent Definitions (.claude/agents/, agents.md) define specialized personas - a code reviewer, a security auditor, a technical writer. They set who the agent is and what role it plays. But they do not teach how to do a specific task. A security auditor agent still needs your organization's specific review checklist. Think of it as a job title: it tells you someone is an accountant, but not how your company's month-end close works.

Skills (.claude/skills/,SKILL.md) define how to do a specific task. They load only when the task matches, and you can have hundreds without context pollution. Think of it as a recipe card: you have a whole box of them, but you only pull out the one you need right now.

 Instruction FilesAgent DefinitionsSkills
Answers the question"How should we work in this project?""What role does this agent play?""How do I do this specific task?"
Loaded whenEvery session, alwaysWhen the role is activatedOnly when the task matches
Scales toA few rules per projectA handful of rolesHundreds of procedures
Knowledge typeConventions and preferencesIdentity and behaviorProcedures and workflows
Portable across toolsNo (format varies by tool)No (format varies by tool)Yes (open standard, 35+ tools)

These three are not competing - they are layers. Instruction files set the project rules. Agent definitions set the role. Skills provide the step-by-step procedures. A well-configured agent might use all three at the same time.

How This Maps to Human Memory

There is a parallel from cognitive science that makes this entire concept easy to remember.

Humans have three distinct types of memory:

  • Semantic memory - Facts you know. "Delhi is the capital of India." You do not remember when or where you learned this - you just know it.
  • Episodic memory - Experiences you remember. "When I visited Delhi last June, it was 45 degrees and I learned to never schedule outdoor meetings after noon." This is tied to a specific time and place.
  • Procedural memory - Things you know how to do. "How to navigate the Delhi Metro during rush hour." You do not think through which line to take, where to switch, or which side of the platform to stand on - you just do it. The procedure is internalized.

AI agent architectures are starting to mirror exactly this:

Your MemoryAgent's EquivalentHow It Works
Semantic (facts you know)RAG and knowledge basesThe agent retrieves facts from a document store. "What is our refund policy?" - it looks it up
Episodic (experiences you remember)Conversation history and logs"What did we discuss yesterday?" - the agent recalls prior interactions
Procedural (things you know how to do)Skill files"Write my status report" - the agent follows your team's specific process

This maps cleanly onto the different ways of giving agents knowledge:

  • RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) is the agent's reference library. It retrieves facts from documents at runtime - policies, specifications, product details. But a library does not teach you a procedure. You can read every book about cycling and still not know how to ride a bike.
  • MCP (Model Context Protocol) gives the agent access to tools - like handing someone a bicycle. But having a bicycle does not mean you know how to ride it, when to shift gears, or which route to take to work. MCP provides the tool. A skill tells the agent when to use it and how.
  • Fine-tuning is like surgically rewiring your muscles and balance so cycling becomes instinct. Powerful, but expensive - and if you want to learn mountain biking next, you have to redo the whole thing.
  • Skills are the riding lessons. Step by step: how to balance, when to brake, how to shift gears on a hill. Written down, easy to update, and you can hand the same instructions to anyone.

In practice they all work together. A skill provides the playbook - "first do this, then check that, then format the result like so." MCP provides the connections. RAG provides the reference material. The skill is the glue that orchestrates when to reach for what, and what to do with the results.

An Open Standard

One more thing that sets skills apart from instruction files likeCLAUDE.md or .cursorrules:  portability.

CLAUDE.md only works in Claude Code..cursorrules only works in Cursor. Each tool has its own format, and they are not interchangeable. Skills follow an open standard published at agentskills.io, originally developed by Anthropic under the Apache 2.0 license. It has been adopted by over 35 platforms including Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI, JetBrains Junie, Databricks Genie Code, Snowflake Cortex Code, Spring AI, Kiro, and many more.

A skill you write for Claude Code works on any platform that supports the spec. Your team member who prefers Cursor or GitHub Copilot can use the exact same skill from the same Git repository. Write once, use everywhere.

A Word on Trust

Skills can include executable scripts in the scripts/ directory. That makes them powerful - but executable code means trust matters. When an agent runs a script from a skill, it runs with your permissions on your machine.

Treat skill installation like any software dependency: read the SKILL.md before installing, check the scripts/ directory if it exists, and prefer skills from known sources. Skills are human-readable by design, which makes them easier to audit than compiled packages - but "easy to read" is not a reason to skip reading it.

Let's See It in Action

Enough theory. Let's create a real skill and see how it changes the experience.

We will build a weekly-status skill for the status report example we have been discussing throughout this blog.

Create the Skill

Where you store a skill determines who can use it:

LocationPathApplies to
EnterpriseManaged settingsAll users in your organization
Personal~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.mdAll your projects
Project.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.mdThis project only
Plugin<plugin>/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.mdWhere plugin is enabled

When skills share the same name across levels, higher-priority locations win: enterprise > personal > project. For most teams, the two you will use daily are personal (your home directory, available everywhere) and project (committed to Git, shared with the team).

We want the team to share this one, so we will use the project directory. Open a terminal (Terminal on macOS/Linux, Command Prompt or PowerShell on Windows) and navigate to your project's root folder.

macOS / Linux:

mkdir -p .claude/skills/weekly-status

Windows (Command Prompt):

mkdir .claude\skills\weekly-status

This creates the folder structure .claude/skills/weekly-status/ inside your project. On Windows, mkdir creates parent directories automatically. On macOS/Linux, the -p flag does the same.

Now create a file called SKILL.md inside that folder. You can use any text editor - VS Code, Notepad, TextEdit, vim, whatever you prefer. The file path should be:

  • macOS / Linux: .claude/skills/weekly-status/SKILL.md
  • Windows: .claude\skills\weekly-status\SKILL.md

Add this content:

---
name: weekly-status
description: >
  Writes the team's weekly status report in the standard format.
  Use when the user asks to write, draft, or prepare a status
  report, weekly update, or weekly summary.
---

# Weekly Status Report

Write a weekly status report covering Monday to Friday of the
current week.

## Report Structure

Follow this exact structure:

### 1. Completed
- List each completed item as a bullet point
- Start each bullet with a past-tense verb (Finished, Fixed,
  Delivered, Migrated, etc.)
- Be specific - include module names, ticket numbers, or
  feature names when the user provides them

### 2. Blockers
- Each blocker must include:
  - What is blocked
  - Severity: [LOW], [MEDIUM], or [HIGH]
  - Owner: who needs to resolve it
- If an item is overdue by more than 2 days, add a [RISK] flag
- If there are no blockers, write "None"

### 3. Next Week
- List planned items as bullet points
- Start each bullet with a verb (Start, Continue, Complete,
  Review, etc.)

### 4. Needs from Leadership
- Always include this section
- If nothing is needed, write "None at this time"

## Formatting Rules
- Bullet points only. No paragraphs.
- Keep each bullet to one or two lines maximum
- No greetings, no sign-offs, no filler
- Use plain text, no markdown formatting in the output

## Before writing, ask the user:
- What did you work on this week?
- Any blockers or things waiting on someone else?
- What is planned for next week?

That is it. The skill is ready.

Use the Skill

Open Claude Code in your project. You have two ways to use it:

Option A - Invoke directly: Type /weekly-status. Claude Code loads the skill and starts the procedure.

Option B - Ask naturally: Type "Write my weekly status report." Claude Code matches your request to the skill description and loads it automatically.

Here is what the interaction looks like:

agent-skill

Notice what the agent did. It did not just fill in a template. It asked for inputs, realized the answers were too brief, and asked follow-up questions to get useful detail - exactly as the skill instructed. Then it applied judgment: it rated the blocker as MEDIUM, linked the dependency between Jira-234 and Jira-345, identified Sam as the owner, and even suggested a leadership follow-up for the unresolved dependency. Every rule in the skill was followed, and the agent reasoned about the specifics of your situation.

The Before and After

AspectBefore Skills After Skills
Writing the reportType a paragraph explaining the format, then your updatesType /weekly-status and answer three questions
Remembering the formatYou memorize 6 rules and hope you get them rightThe skill remembers them
Blocker severity ratingsYou remember to add them (sometimes)Always included
Risk flags on overdue itemsYou calculate this yourselfFlagged automatically
"Needs from Leadership" sectionForgotten half the timeAlways included
Sharing with the teamSend a notes file, hope everyone stays in syncCommit the skill to Git
Updating the formatEdit every copy, notify everyoneEdit one SKILL.md, push to Git
Context window impactIf in CLAUDE.md: loaded every session, even when irrelevantLoaded only when you ask for a status report

What Else Can You Build?

The weekly-status skill we built uses only aSKILL.md file. But remember the optional folders - scripts/, references/, assets/? That is where things get interesting. Here are some ideas - inspired by real problems teams deal with every day - to show you what becomes possible.

For Developers

pr-summary - Summarize pull requests in SAP language

Across lines of business, PR review latency - not coding - is the dominant bottleneck. Review cycles span days because reviewers have to read every file to understand the scope. This skill changes that.

  • scripts/ runs git diff and extracts the list of changed files, grouped by type (CDS models, service definitions, UI5 views, config files)
  • references/ contains your team's PR description standards and SAP-specific terminology guide (what a CDS annotation change means, what an OData projection change implies)
  • SKILL.md instructs the agent to summarize the diff in functional terms - not "changed 3 files" but "Adds a new OData entity projection in the CAP CDS model and updates the corresponding Fiori Elements list report configuration." Reviewers grasp the scope in seconds instead of reading every file

cap-scaffold - Scaffold a full-stack CAP + Fiori/UI5 project

Setting up a new CAP project correctly - with CDS data models, OData service definitions, a Fiori/UI5 front-end, XSUAA authentication, CI/CD pipeline, and monitoring - takes days of stitching pieces together. This skill turns it into a conversation.

  • assets/ holds templates: CDS entity definitions, Fiori Elements manifest.json, xs-security.json for XSUAA, GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow, Dockerfile
  • references/ has SAP's Golden Path architecture guidelines, approved library lists, and naming conventions per LoB
  • SKILL.md asks what you are building ("a Books catalog with list-detail UI"), then scaffolds the entire project from templates, wires up authentication, sets up the CI pipeline, and generates a README explaining every architectural choice it made

ci-triage - Diagnose CI failures in seconds

When CI pipelines fail, developers spend time sifting through logs only to find it was an environment or config issue, not a code problem. This skill triages failures automatically.

  • scripts/ fetches the CI log output, parses error messages and stack traces, and extracts the failing test or build step
  • references/ contains a catalog of common failure patterns your team has seen before (dependency version conflicts, environment drift, flaky tests) with known fixes
  • SKILL.md reads the parsed output, matches it against known patterns, and produces a diagnosis: "Test failure in OrderService.test.js - NullPointerException: new field OrderDate not initialized in test fixture. Suggested fix: provide default value in test data setup." Root cause and fix, not just a red X

compliance-check - Shift-left security and privacy review

Teams operates under non-negotiable constraints: secure coding standards, GDPR data privacy, export controls, OSS license hygiene. Today, violations are found late - during formal security reviews - creating friction and rework. This skill catches them at the point of coding.

  • scripts/ runs static analysis on the diff - checks for hardcoded credentials, insecure deserialization, weak cryptography, and banned functions. A second script scans package.json or pom.xml or pyproject.toml for new dependencies and checks them against an allow-list of approved OSS licenses
  • references/ contains SAP's secure coding rules by category (security, privacy, licensing, architecture) and the current approved license list (MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD)
  • SKILL.md orchestrates a two-pass review: deterministic checks first (scripts), then contextual AI analysis of data flow patterns the scripts cannot catch - like a function that reads personal data in one module and logs it unmasked in another. Output is a structured compliance summary categorized by severity and type

For Business Users

client-proposal - Draft a client proposal that follows your company's playbook

Every sales team has a format: executive summary first, then pain points, proposed solution, pricing table, case studies, timeline. But when you ask an AI to "write a proposal," you get a generic template that misses your company's structure, tone, and pricing rules. This skill knows your playbook.

  • assets/ holds the proposal template with your standard sections, pricing table format, and case study layouts
  • references/ contains your pricing tiers, approved discount thresholds, service descriptions, and a library of past case studies tagged by industry and deal size
  • SKILL.md walks you through: gather client requirements, select the most relevant case studies from the library, draft each section in your company's tone, fill in the pricing table with the correct tier, and flag if any proposed discount exceeds the threshold that needs manager approval

learning-plan - Personalized weekly study plan for professional development

Your company offers a catalog of courses for upskilling - maybe it is cloud certifications, AI fundamentals, leadership development, or technical role transitions. The courses exist, but employees do not know what to take, in what order, within their available time. This skill turns a sprawling course catalog into a weekly plan that fits your schedule.

  • scripts/ calculates time budgets - splits multi-hour courses into 25-30 minute sessions, sequences them across weeks to respect your weekly hour cap (e.g., 3 hours/week), and flags prerequisites
  • references/ has the course catalog with durations, difficulty levels, and prerequisite chains for each learning path
  • SKILL.md asks three questions: what you want to learn, what you already know, and how many hours per week you can dedicate. Then it generates a week-by-week learning plan with specific sessions, estimated completion dates, and checkpoints to verify you are retaining what you learned

sales-scheme - Analyze and optimize rebate programs

Sales schemes are shared via PDFs, emails, and circulars. Manual interpretation leads to inconsistent rebate decisions, and there is no visibility into whether a scheme is actually effective. This skill brings structure and data to the process.

  • scripts/ parses scheme documents (discounts, slabs, conditions), links them with sales data, and calculates which customers are close to achieving the next slab threshold
  • references/ contains the current scheme rules, historical performance data, and the rebate calculation logic
  • assets/ has the recommendation report template with sections for current performance, suggested rebate adjustments, and smart nudges ("Customer X is close to the next slab - an increase of Y units would unlock a higher rebate")
  • SKILL.md guides you through: upload the scheme, review the AI's analysis of what is working and what is not, and get specific recommendations - optimized rebate percentages, credit note estimates, and customer-level nudges to drive behavior before month-end

onboarding-checklist - Personalized new hire setup

Every new hire needs IT access, HR paperwork, team introductions, tool setup, and a training schedule. But onboarding differs by role - an engineer's first week looks nothing like a sales rep's. Most companies have a checklist somewhere, but it is generic, outdated, or buried in a wiki nobody reads. This skill generates a personalized plan.

  • assets/ has templates for IT setup requests, HR forms, training schedules, and first-week calendars
  • references/ has role-specific guides (engineering onboarding includes dev environment setup and repo access; sales onboarding includes CRM training and territory briefing)
  • SKILL.md asks for the new hire's name, role, team, and start date, then generates a personalized checklist with specific tasks, deadlines relative to the start date, links to each form or system, and the name of who to contact for each step

Each of these examples uses the same simple building blocks: aSKILL.md for instructions, optional scripts for automation, optional references for context, and optional assets for templates. The format is the same. The possibilities are not.

Blog Series

BlogTopicStatus
Blog 1Calling Your First LLM on SAP AI CorePublished
Blog 2Structured Output and Your First AgentPublished
Blog 3From Terminal to Browser - A Chat UIPublished
Blog 4Evaluation with MLflowPublished

entry.title: Scope items for Contract and Lease Management in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi all,

We are investigating different scope items for CLM in the Public Cloud. We are not intending to use the integration with Planon yet. However it seems that some of the scope items cannot be used since they need the Planon integration to be set-up. I could not find this very clearly stated in the scope items.

It mainly concerns these scope items:

- Real Estate Cost Settlement (6N8)

- Intercompany Lease Accounting – Group Ledger IFRS (7G4)

If I check the scope items, these are not flagged as integration relevant.

We are able to create Usable and Rentable objects, which is fine, since we capture some data in the objects (like measurements). These objects can be assigned to the Lease contracts. However if we activate the Real Estate Cost Settlement, we can also create Service Groups, but here is the field Building/Land required. We do not have this object and can also not create it.

Can you please tell us if integration with Planon is needed for these scope items and why we cannot use the scope items without Planon.

Thanks in advance,

Jeroen

entry.title: Email + Web Channel Translations via API?
entry.content:
Hi, 

On https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_EMARSYS/e6abdb1908754ed8aca7e3e7228407f3/fdf4a2be74c110148c30a4cdeb5ab4a6.html it is mentioned that an add-on exists for Emarsys to enable automated translations for emails, either via SAP AI, or via Deepl or Smartling.

I could find https://api.sap.com/api/SAPEmarsysCoreEmailCampaigns/path/updateEmailCampaign / https://dev.emarsys.com/docs/core-api-reference/5osux89c8np5a-update-an-email-campaign but the field "html body" mentions "Note: Not available for template-based campaigns." , so I am doubting whether this could be used or not. But anyway via this method the marketeer would probably still need to exit from Emarsys and go somewhere else to trigger the translations via API? 

 

And what about Web Channel content, for that I can not really find anything on automatic translation options?

entry.title: Re: ABAP RAP Custom Entity with Parameter not allowed
entry.content:
Actually I got above error in the UI, when i try to use Parametrized CDS in the backend extensibility.

Solution:
1. Actually I expose the service as OData V2 - UI. Refer below img 



2. Check the manifest.json file in the BTP. (SAP Business application studio --> Open
your project file --> webApp --> search for manifest.json file).

Search for 'Pages-->ListReport' in the manifest.json. Refer the below image and the marked fields should match your exposed CDS name.
Example: In service binding: ZR_PP_DIFF_PERCENT_CATEGORYSet
                   In BTP's manifest.json file: ZR_PP_DIFF_PERCENT_CATEGORYSet
Both should be same. Don't get confuse! how you get the suffix 'set' next to 'CDS name'. It is default behaviour of OData V2

3. Redeploy the application in BTP.

entry.title: Re: Integration of SAP public cloud system with DATEV
entry.content:
Good day @Naveenk_S42024 

Thank you for your question.

I am not aware of the Public Cloud providing a native standard integration that directly exports G/L data into DATEV compliant CSV or ASCII format with built in mapping for SKR03 or SKR04, DATEV tax keys, and DATEV cost centers.

The Public Cloud provides released data access objects that can cover the core extraction scope, for example:

  • G/L line item data via I_GLAccountLineItem or I_JournalEntryItemCube
  • Chart of accounts and G/L master data via I_GLAccount and related APIs/views
  • Cost center data via cost center APIs and mass maintenance APIs

So, SAP gives you the source data access, but not a delivered DATEV formatter or DATEV specific mapper in Public Cloud.

Regarding CoA mapping to SKR03 or SKR04 you mentioned, the Public Cloud does not provide a function that automatically maps your internal chart of accounts to DATEV SKR03 or SKR04 during export. This would typically need to be maintained in a custom middleware or transformation layer. Accounting master and transaction data is only exposed through released interfaces by SAP.

Same goes for Tax key translation to DATEV tax keys and Cost center mapping to DATEV Kostenstellen.

I recommend you design your architecture to:

  1. Extract accounting data from S4/HANA Public Cloud using released APIs or CDS based extraction.
  2. Send it to a middleware or integration layer, such as SAP Integration Suite or a partner solution.
  3. Perform your required mapping there.
  4. Generate the final DATEV compliant CSV or ASCII file in that external layer.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/377bfe5702c80f70e10000000a44147b.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: #CoE#TAIT What functionality is available to synchron...
entry.content:
Good day @davidhe79 

Thank you for your question.

There is not separate standard feature to synchronize backend system time with the Maintenance Assistant app clock. The backend runs on UTC by design, while time display can depend on the your Fiori time zone settings. So, the right place for your customer to check is the Language and Region/time zone setting rather than a system time synchronisation function.

Here are references for more guidance

3514300 - Time zone Explanation - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/1fbd919788be494182c3af4ca3c8f97c.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Looking for connection within the SAP space
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

My name is Nosipho, and I’m an SAP Functional Consultant specializing in MM and SD, with hands-on experience in SAP ECC system, and currently upgrading my skills for S/4HANA implementations.

Over the course of my work, I’ve been involved in supporting and improving end-to-end procurement and sales processes, including areas such as purchasing, inventory management, pricing, billing, and cross‑module integration between MM, SD, and FI.

I joined the SAP Community to connect with fellow MM/SD professionals, learn from real project experiences, and stay up to date with the latest developments in S/4HANA, especially around:

  • Logistics process simplifications in S/4HANA
  • Fiori apps for MM and SD
  • Output Management
  • Integration and extension topics (incl. SAP BTP at a conceptual level)

I strongly believe that continuous learning and knowledge sharing are key in the SAP world, and I’m looking forward to actively contributing, asking questions, and exchanging ideas with this community.

If you work in SAP MM, SD, Logistics, or S/4HANA transformation projects, I’d be happy to connect and learn from your experience as well.

Looking forward to the discussions!

Best regards,

Nosipho

entry.title: New Course Launch: Selling SAP CIAM
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce the launch of our new course, Selling SAP CIAM, designed specifically for sales professionals who want to confidently position and sell SAP’s Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) solutions.

In today’s digital economy, customer experience and trust are everything. Organizations need secure, seamless ways to manage customer identities while meeting growing expectations around privacy and personalization. SAP CIAM plays a critical role in helping businesses achieve this balance—and now, you can learn how to sell it effectively.

What This Course Is About

Selling SAP CIAM empowers sales teams with the knowledge and skills they need to succeed in CIAM-focused conversations. The course bridges the gap between technical capabilities and business value, enabling you to speak the language of both IT and business stakeholders.

You’ll gain a strong understanding of:

  • Customer identity challenges faced by modern organizations
  • Common customer use cases for SAP CIAM across industries
  • Key value propositions that resonate with business and technical decision-makers
  • How to position SAP CIAM as a solution that enhances security, compliance, and customer experience

Rather than focusing purely on features, the course emphasizes understanding your customer’s needs and aligning SAP CIAM capabilities to real-world business outcomes.

Who Should Attend

This course is ideal for:

  • Sales executives and account managers
  • Pre-sales and solution advisors
  • Anyone responsible for positioning or selling SAP CIAM solutions

Whether you’re new to CIAM or looking to sharpen your sales message, this course provides practical insights you can immediately apply in customer interactions.

Get Started

If you’re ready to strengthen your CIAM sales expertise and elevate your customer conversations, Selling SAP CIAM is the perfect next step. Stay tuned for enrollment details and take the opportunity to position yourself at the forefront of identity-driven selling.

entry.title: Re: SAP B1 GRPO posting wrong amount to Inventory acct fo...
entry.content:
Hi,

system is taking the last recorded avg. price and quantity (in your case 21.750 for 1pcs) and adding to it the new price (22.250 and 1pcs) and using those values for calculations. This results in 44.000 being divided by 2 = 22.000.

BR,

Matija

entry.title: Re: Stock replication trigger behavior in SAP_COM_0447
entry.content: Hi Dawid, Thanks for your detailed explanation and references. After reviewing the shared links and testing in our system, we have a few follow-up points: We understand that SAP_COM_0447 (Material Stock Replicate) is not event-driven. However, even when a material document is created (e.g. GR/GI), we observed that the corresponding notification was not received in our integration layer. → Could there be additional configuration or prerequisites required for triggering MaterialDocumentCreateNotification_Async_Out? Regarding the event-driven approach: → Which event ID / business event should be used in standard to capture stock-relevant changes (GR, GI, transfer posting)? We would appreciate clarification on the exact event configuration in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. entry.title: Cost Center Based GL Account Mapping - SAP Payroll
entry.content:
Introduction:

One of my clients had a requirement to split the General Ledger Account Bookings as Recoverable Costs and Non-Recoverable Costs. Certain Employees costs should be posted in Recoverable GL and Certain in Non-Recoverable GL. The Employees were differentiated using Recoverable and Non - Recoverable Cost Centers.

The Configuration :

This can be achieved during Standard SAP Payroll Functionality. Before we dive in to the logic to split the GL Postings , We should create two Different Employee Determination Groups. The Employee Determination Groups are used during assignment of GL Accounts.

Steps to Create Employee Group for Account Determination :

SPRO ->SAP  Reference IMG -> Payroll-> Click on Respective Location Payroll Node -> Reporting for Posting Payroll Results to Accounting -> Activities in the HR System -> Employee Grouping and Symbolic Accounts -> Define Employee Grouping Account Determination -> Define Values

Building the Decision Tree to Assign the Employee Grouping for Account Determination Values :

We are leveraging the Standard Feature Functionaliy in SAP Payroll to achiee.The PPMOD Feature helps us build a decision tree to assing the EG Group.

Start with Company Code  (BUKRS) -> Create a Sub - Decision -> KOSTL (Cost Center) -> Assign EG Value 

Sub-Decisions can be added as per Business Choice , Here my Decision Tree is based on Company Code -> Employee Group -> Cost Center

 

Make sure to add EG Value to the Otherwise and remember to activate the Decision Tree

That's all it requires, A Simple yet very effective Solution using Feature Functionality.

Conclusion: 

By leveraging the standard feature PPMOD within SAP Payroll, the requirement to split General Ledger postings into Recoverable and Non-Recoverable costs can be handled cleanly without complex custom development.

This approach offers a few clear advantages. It keeps the solution aligned with standard SAP functionality, making it easier to maintain and transport across environments. It also ensures flexibility—any future changes to cost center structures or classification logic can be handled by simply updating the decision tree in the feature configuration rather than modifying code.

In essence, by combining cost center-based logic with Employee Determination Groups and the feature decision tree, organizations can achieve accurate financial segregation of payroll costs in a scalable and efficient manner.

entry.title: Re: Data persistence in Datasphere
entry.content: If you count(*) on a view it will not "open" the association and also left outer joins are not opened. If you persist the view it must as I see it open all associations and left outer joins and that is where you could have a 1:n relation that explodes your data

entry.title: Re: Change Leading Product / Version of syst
entry.content:
Unfortunately, it is not possible to change the leading product and version for an existing system.
 
To update the leading product and version of a system, you will need to create a new system with new license keys for the correct Leading Product & Version SAP S4HANA 2021 / SAP S4HANA 2021 in the License Keys application.

For more detailed information, please refer to the KBAs below:
 
2241656 - How to view or maintain on-premise System Data - SAP for Me

The following system data cannot be maintained in the System Data application. Instead, use the License Keys application. See the best practices information in KBA 2531837 (SAP Business Suite products) and KBA 2446681 (BusinessObjects products).

  • System Name (Note: If system has license keys, use the License Keys application. For systems without license keys, System Name can be changed in System Data application)
  • Product Name
  • Product Version
  • System Type (test, dev, prod)

2531837 - Best Practices of editing SAP Business Suite systems and license keys - SAP for Me

What types of changes should not (or cannot) be made to an existing systems or license keys?
If you need to change any of the following, you must create a new system with new license keys in the License Keys application. So in cases where you have a new deployment of the software, you should create a new system instead of editing an existing system. Please do not edit an existing system or license key for the following types of changes:

  • Product Name and/or Version changed  
  • Operating System changed
  • System Type or Development Phase changed (e.g. License Type changes from Test, Development, Production)
  • Database changed - Note: As mentioned above, only change the database for an existing system if all license keys under that system are to be changed to the new database. If you plan to use the old database for some systems and new data for others, you should create separate systems for each database. 
  • 3-character System ID cannot be changed (a new system needs to be created)

For instructions on how to request a license for S/4HANA, please refer to KBA 2366280 - How to request a license key for SAP S/4HANA (all versions) - SAP for Me

If you have any questions or need further assistance, please feel free to contact us.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Enable ssl in Hana replication for DR
entry.content:
Hi,

SSL is enabled by default.

So if you want to turn it on explicitly , you can set it online with command :

  • ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('global.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('system_replication_communication','enable_ssl') = 'on' WITH RECONFIGURE

Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: Re: Email Template not generating in Q-System after Softw...
entry.content:
Good day @arnap_silea16cc 

Thank you for your question.

A gap you might have after you have transported a custom email template with Export Software Collection and Import Collection is not a separate activation in Q, but the setup for your business relevant object and output type in Output Parameter Determination. A template can be visible in Maintain Email Templates and still not be used to generate email if you miss the required config in output management. To ensure that is not the case, please check these points first in Q:

  1. Assign Email Templates (ID 101945) to the business context. If the template was imported but not properly assigned to the relevant business context, it may appear in Maintain Email Templates but not be valid in Output Parameter Determination. See KBA 3359258 for config steps.                                                                                     
  2. Open Output Parameter Determination for the affected application object and output type, verify the channel is Email, your custom email template is selected and you have maintained your Sender email in Email Settings. See KBA 2676588 on Email Setting steps and its impacts.                                                                                       
  3. There is usually no separate activation step in Maintain Email Templates itself after you have completed the import. The relevant action is for you to run Check and Activate in Output Parameter Determination (in Q) if the rule is inconsistent or inactive. See KBA 2843926 for output rules.

So, in summary, the item commonly missed is the business context and OPD assignment, not the transport of the template object itself. The checks mentioned above should be carried out if the template is visible in Q but emails are no longer generated. 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP B1 GRPO posting wrong amount to Inventory acct fo...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

sorry, I focused on the highlighted row. Can you please tell me what documents PD2600147 and PD2600148 are? Seems to me those are cancellation documents (looking from Cumulative Qty column). If so, then the explanation is simple, Trans. Value is not showing the new item cost, but rather increase/decrease of inventory value happening with that transaction. To explain:

PD2500082 added to stock 21.000 value, which was removed with DN2500034

PD 2600109 needed to have avg. cost of 21.750, and since there was no qty on stock it achieved this by setting the value to that amount. This value remained as no items were sold (delivered) before the next GRPO

PD 2600133 by calculations had to increase the inventory value to a total of 44.000, and to achieve that it needed the difference 44.000 - 21.750 = 22.250 added to your stock value account (as shown in Trans Value).

Now, when you decided to cancel those GRPOs (PD2600147 and PD2600148), the system removed for each item its calculated value of 22.000 ultimately ending up with 0 stock value and 0 qty.

 

BR,

Matija

entry.title: Re: Observations on NSE Implementation for CDPOS Table in...
entry.content:
Hello,

Warm page loadable NSE data are loaded into these "warm" heap allocators in HANA when accessed :

  • Pool/CS/BufferPage
  • Pool/CS/BufferCachePageProvider:Controlblock
  • Pool/CS/BufferCachePageProvider:DataPage

While the table columns and pages themselves are present in the buffer cache, the fetch requests, Calculations and joins that are done on the CDPOS table on a statement level, will be populated under the "Statement & Intermediate Results" heap allocators like "Pool/JoinEvaluator/ . . " for example and thus these allocators are stored in "hot" main memory until the statement finishes.

The LOAD_UNIT column in the TABLE_COLUMNS view will show which of the CDPOS columns are stored in "PAGE" loadable units or not, since not every column may have been configured as page loadable. Any non-page loadable columns will be loaded into the hot memory by default under "Pool/Columnstore/ . . ." .

SQL: "HANA_Tables_ColumnStore_TableSize_2.00.030+" (SAP Note 1969700) reports the NSE-related memory consumption in column PAGED_GB.

See section '12. How can I calculate the memory consumption of NSE enabled tables?' - 2799997 - FAQ: SAP HANA Native Storage Extension (NSE)

You can remove the table from NSE at any time via command : ALTER TABLE "<table_name>" DEFAULT LOADABLE;

Kind Regards
Simon
